title: Sliding contact creates universal self-affine fractal surfaces
authors: Ruibin Xu, Haohao Ren, Adriane Clerc, Guilhem Mollon, Wenbo Sheng, Feng Zhou, B. N. J. Persson
abstract: Surface roughness evolves during sliding, a process known as run-in, and the resulting topography controls friction, leakage, and failure from machines to geological faults. Yet the physical rule selecting this state remains unclear. We show that metals, rocks, and glasses develop universal self-similar roughness at short wavelengths, while retaining a material-dependent roll-off. A two-process model explains this behavior: junction formation and rupture drive universal roughening, whereas larger-scale deformation and/or fracture limit its growth.
summary: This work investigates how surface roughness evolves during sliding contact, finding that the process exhibits universal self-similar scaling at small scales due to junction dynamics. This roughening is counteracted by a material-specific smoothing mechanism (like plastic deformation), leading to a characteristic roll-off in the surface's power spectrum. The findings establish a quantitative, two-process model for wear and friction.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental physical rule governing the final, steady-state roughness of surfaces after sliding contact (run-in), a process critical for understanding friction and wear.
main_result: The roughness exhibits universal self-affine scaling ($H \approx 1$) at short wavelengths, driven by junction formation/rupture, while a material-dependent roll-off at long wavelengths is set by competing smoothing mechanisms like plastic deformation or fracture.
method: The study combines experimental measurements of surface topography (PSD analysis) with theoretical modeling based on a junction-transfer process.
model_or_system: The system is a sliding contact between various materials (metals, rocks, glasses). The model posits that roughness evolves via a competition between a universal roughening process (junction dynamics) and a material-specific smoothing process (plasticity/fracture).
key_observables: Self-affine roughness characterized by the Hurst exponent ($H$), and the power spectral density (PSD) showing distinct short-wavelength ($H=1$) and long-wavelength (material-dependent roll-off) regimes.
important_parameters: The competition between junction formation/rupture (roughening) and plastic deformation/fracture (smoothing) defines the characteristic roll-off wavenumber ($q_r$).
main_assumptions: The roughening mechanism is modeled by the random addition/removal of 'blocks' at microscopic junctions, and the steady state requires an additional smoothing mechanism beyond the junction dynamics to explain the finite roll-off.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the emergence of universal self-affine topography, showing PSDs collapsing onto an $H=1$ regime at short scales, while also demonstrating the suppression of this scaling under boundary lubrication.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical problem, presents experimental evidence showing universal short-scale scaling, develops a two-process model explaining the competition between junction-driven roughening and material-limited smoothing, and validates this model using simulations.
why_interesting: The concept of universal scaling laws emerging from microscopic, non-equilibrium processes (like junction dynamics) that are then arrested by macroscopic material failure mechanisms is relevant to non-equilibrium statistical physics and pattern formation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Emergence of universal self-affine surface topography during sliding. (a) Schematic of the reciprocating sliding experiment and the resulting topographical evolution. Regardless of the initial roughness, the surfaces evolve toward a common steady state with universal self-affine characteristics. (b) Evolution of the 1D surface roughness PSD C1D(q) for a smooth steel slider sliding on a smooth steel substrate under dry reciprocating conditions. PSDs are computed from height profiles measured along the y direction, perpendicular to the sliding direction. The last two curves (12.0 m and 50.4 m) nearly coincide, indicating that run-in is completed after ∼12 m. The dashed line indicates...

FIG. 2. Stochastic junction-transfer model reproducing the measured self-affine roughness. (a) A 2048×2048 surface generated after N ≈1011 steps of block addition and removal (left, side view; right, top view). (b) Two-dimensional PSDs of the generated surfaces after N steps, plotted as a function of qa, where q is the wavenumber and a is the block size; the roll-off shifts to smaller q as N increases. (c) Comparison between the line topography of a steel surface after run-in (blue) and a line profile extracted from the simulated surface in (a) (red), showing similar surface features within the measured range.

FIG. 3. Schematic illustration of the two-process mechanism proposed in this study. At large length scales (left), sliding promotes smoothing through plastic deformation and/or fracture of asperities. At small length scales (right), repeated junction formation and rupture drive discrete material-transfer events that generate multiscale roughening and H = 1 regime. The competition between roughening and smoothing defines a roll-off wavenumber qr, which separates the universal short-wavelength regime from the material-dependent long-wavelength morphology.
